verses.text: Naomi had a kinsman of her husband's, a mighty man of wealth, of the family of Elimelech; and his name was Boaz. Ruth the Moabitess said unto Naomi, Let me now go to the field, and glean ears of corn after him in whose sight I shall find grace. She said unto her, Go, my daughter. She went, and came, and gleaned in the field after the reapers: and her hap was to light on a part of the field belonging unto Boaz, who was of the kindred of Elimelech. Guidance: The narrator introduces Boaz with three details: kinsman of the dead husband, wealthy, named. The Hebrew phrase 'mighty man of wealth' is gibbor hayil — the same expression used of military heroes. Boaz is rich, capable, and connected to Elimelech's family. Ruth proposes gleaning (the legal right of widows, orphans, and foreigners under Lev 19:9-10) and Naomi sends her. 'Her hap was to light on…' — the narrator deadpans the providence. From Ruth's angle it is random; from the narrator's it is anything but. The book's theology of God's hand is everywhere quiet: never a thunderbolt, always a coincidence that turns out to be the long arm of mercy.

Behold, Boaz came from Beth-lehem, and said unto the reapers, The LORD be with you. And they answered him, The LORD bless thee. Guidance: Boaz greets his workers with the LORD's name and they answer in kind. A small ordinary blessing — but listen for what is happening in the wider book of Judges: 'In those days every man did that which was right in his own eyes.' Here is a man whose first words to his employees invoke the LORD and whose employees answer with the same God's blessing. A faithful workplace in a faithless era. The little detail tells us everything we need to know about Boaz's character before he ever speaks to Ruth.

Then said Boaz unto his servant that was set over the reapers, Whose damsel is this? The servant that was set over the reapers answered and said, It is the Moabitish damsel that came back with Naomi out of the country of Moab: and she said, I pray you, let me glean and gather after the reapers among the sheaves: so she came, and hath continued even from the morning until now, that she tarried a little in the house. Guidance: Boaz notices Ruth and asks 'whose damsel?' — not yet 'who is she?' but to whose household does she belong, an inquiry shaped by Israelite social structures. The foreman's report is precise: she is the Moabitess who came back with Naomi, asked politely to glean, has been working hard since morning with only a short break. The portrait is of a quiet, hardworking, honest young woman. The community has been watching her; the foreman knows her story. Faithful work in a small town gets noticed even when the worker thinks she is invisible.

Then said Boaz unto Ruth, Hearest thou not, my daughter? Go not to glean in another field, neither go from hence, but abide here fast by my maidens: let thine eyes be on the field that they do reap, and go thou after them: have I not charged the young men that they shall not touch thee? and when thou art athirst, go unto the vessels, and drink of that which the young men have drawn. Guidance: Boaz speaks to Ruth directly. 'My daughter' — protective, generational, paternal. He extends four kindnesses: stay in my field, glean with my maidens, my men will not touch you, you have access to my water. He addresses the female vulnerability of a foreign woman gleaning alone — sexual harassment and ethnic abuse were live risks. He builds a perimeter of safety with one short paragraph. Pastoral leadership in a few sentences: name the danger, neutralize the danger, give the access.

Then she fell on her face, and bowed herself to the ground, and said unto him, Why have I found grace in thine eyes, that thou shouldest take knowledge of me, seeing I am a stranger? Guidance: Ruth's instinctive response to undeserved kindness is to fall on her face. The Hebrew word for 'stranger' is nokriyyah — emphasizing foreignness, even more than just 'sojourner.' Her question is the gospel question: 'why grace?' She does not assume she is owed; she does not bristle that the law guarantees her gleaning rights. She just kneels. Humility recognizes mercy as a gift even when there is also a legal entitlement. The chapter's grace begins with a foreign widow on her face.

Boaz answered and said unto her, It hath fully been shewed me, all that thou hast done unto thy mother in law since the death of thine husband: and how thou hast left thy father and thy mother, and the land of thy nativity, and art come unto a people which thou knewest not heretofore. The LORD recompense thy work, and a full reward be given thee of the LORD God of Israel, under whose wings thou art come to trust. Guidance: Boaz answers her question with the source: he knows her story. Bethlehem is small, and Ruth's kindness to Naomi is the talk of the town. He echoes Gen 12:1 — Ruth, like Abraham, has left father, mother, and homeland for the LORD's people. Boaz's blessing is the chapter's deepest image: 'under whose wings thou art come to trust.' The verb 'come to trust' is the same root as 'refuge.' He uses the eagle-wings metaphor of Deut 32:11 — God shelters Israel under His pinions. Boaz tells Ruth she has done not only what the law required but what no foreign woman is asked to do: she has taken refuge in the God of Israel. He prays the LORD's full reward; chapter four will reveal that Boaz himself becomes part of the reward.

The LORD recompense thy work, and a full reward be given thee of the LORD God of Israel, under whose wings thou art come to trust. The verb 'come to trust' is the same root as 'refuge.' He uses the eagle-wings metaphor of Deut 32:11 — God shelters Israel under His pinions. Then she said, Let me find favour in thy sight, my lord; for that thou hast comforted me, and for that thou hast spoken friendly unto thine handmaid, though I be not like unto one of thine handmaidens. Guidance: She thanks him with a humbling clause: 'though I be not like unto one of thine handmaidens.' She does not pretend to be a peer. The Hebrew 'spoken friendly' is literally 'spoken to the heart' — the same phrase used in Gen 50:21 when Joseph reassured his brothers. Boaz's words reached past the foreman's report into the place where she had felt only emptiness. Comfort precedes provision in this chapter. Ruth's bag is still empty; her heart is already fuller.

Boaz said unto her, At mealtime come thou hither, and eat of the bread, and dip thy morsel in the vinegar. She sat beside the reapers: and he reached her parched corn, and she did eat, and was sufficed, and left. Guidance: At noon Boaz invites her to the worker's table — not the boss's table, but the workers' — and personally hands her parched roasted grain. The word 'sufficed' is the same Hebrew used of the manna in the wilderness — the LORD's portion that always equals the need. She eats so much she has leftovers, which she will take home to Naomi (v18). One meal feeds two widows. The deeper image: the man who will become her redeemer feeds her at his table before she even knows what 'redeemer' will mean. Christ's pattern is here in seed.

When she was risen up to glean, Boaz commanded his young men, saying, Let her glean even among the sheaves, and reproach her not: and let fall also some of the handfuls of purpose for her, and leave them, that she may glean them, and rebuke her not. Guidance: After lunch, Boaz gives the chapter's most generous order — to his foremen, out of Ruth's hearing. Drop handfuls 'of purpose' for her. The grace must look accidental from her angle so she keeps her dignity. The young men obey. The chapter's covert generosity is more characterful than the public courtesy. Some mercies are designed never to be discovered as mercies, because the giver knows the receiver would be embarrassed by the visibility of the gift. Boaz invents 'planned coincidence' in scripture.

So she gleaned in the field until even, and beat out that she had gleaned: and it was about an ephah of barley. She took it up, and went into the city: and her mother in law saw what she had gleaned: and she brought forth, and gave to her that she had reserved after she was sufficed. Guidance: An ephah of barley — roughly twenty-two quarts, perhaps thirty pounds. A normal day's gleaning might be a fraction of this. Naomi sees the haul and immediately knows something extraordinary has happened. Then Ruth pulls out the lunch leftovers — Boaz's gift wrapped in a napkin. The widow who said she came home empty (1:21) finds dinner appearing on her table. God's reversal often arrives quietly through the work of someone else's hands.

Her mother in law said unto her, Where hast thou gleaned to day? and where wroughtest thou? blessed be he that did take knowledge of thee. She shewed her mother in law with whom she had wrought, and said, The man's name with whom I wrought to day is Boaz. Naomi said unto her daughter in law, Blessed be he of the LORD, who hath not left off his kindness to the living and to the dead. Naomi said unto her, The man is near of kin unto us, one of our next kinsmen. Guidance: Naomi's prayer is the chapter's quiet turning point. 'Blessed be he of the LORD, who hath not left off his kindness to the living and to the dead.' For the first time since chapter 1, Naomi names the LORD's hesed — and credits Him with the day's grace. Then she drops a fact Ruth did not know: Boaz is a near kinsman, a goel — a kinsman-redeemer with the legal right to buy back family land and even marry a childless widow (Lev 25:25, Deut 25:5-10). The Hebrew word the chapter has not yet spoken aloud is now in the air. The widow who came home calling herself Mara is starting to remember the LORD by His pleasanter name.

Then she drops a fact Ruth did not know: Boaz is a near kinsman, a goel — a kinsman-redeemer with the legal right to buy back family land and even marry a childless widow (Lev 25:25, Deut 25:5-10). Ruth the Moabitess said, He said unto me also, Thou shalt keep fast by my young men, until they have ended all my harvest. Naomi said unto Ruth her daughter in law, It is good, my daughter, that thou go out with his maidens, that they meet thee not in any other field. So she kept fast by the maidens of Boaz to glean unto the end of barley harvest and of wheat harvest; and dwelt with her mother in law. Guidance: Naomi gently corrects Ruth's report — Boaz said 'maidens,' not 'young men'; the chaperonage was women, not men. Even paraphrasing in a household needs precision. The barley harvest runs about a month into the wheat harvest — two more months of food and safety. Ruth dwells with her mother-in-law. The book closes the chapter with a scene of two widows under one roof, the cupboards filling, the worry-lines easing. The third chapter will move from the field to a riskier scene at the threshing floor; for now, peace in the small hours.
